title: Sudden Blindness in One Eye in Dogs: Causes and Emergency Guide
meta_desc: Sudden vision loss in one eye is a veterinary emergency. Learn about causes, why immediate care is critical, and how a photo helps triage.
content_html:
TL;DR: Sudden blindness in one eye is a high-priority veterinary emergency that requires immediate medical attention to manage pain and prevent permanent vision loss.

What are the signs and causes of sudden blindness in one eye for dogs?

Sudden blindness in one eye, or unilateral vision loss, occurs when a dog rapidly loses the ability to see through one eye. Common signs and causes include:

  • Bumping into furniture or walls on one side
  • A dilated pupil that does not shrink when exposed to light
  • Visible cloudiness in the eye
  • Underlying issues such as sudden pressure changes, retinal detachment, or inflammation

Is sudden blindness in one eye a veterinary emergency?

Urgency Level: High. Yes, sudden vision loss is a critical medical emergency. Seeking immediate care is essential because:

  • Causes like acute glaucoma or anterior uveitis are extremely painful for your pet.
  • Permanent, irreversible blindness can occur within just a few hours.
  • Immediate veterinary intervention is the only way to manage pain and potentially save your dog's sight.

How can taking a photo of my dog's eyes help with the triage process?

Providing a clear photo when you call an emergency vet is vital for triage. A photo helps the medical team prepare for your arrival by identifying:

  • The size and responsiveness of the pupils
  • The color of the eye and the presence of cloudiness
  • Any visible discharge or physical abnormalities
  • The severity of symptoms to help prioritize your pet's care upon arrival

Clinical Context (Merck Veterinary Manual)

Sudden blindness in one eye in dogs can occur due to various ophthalmic and central nervous system diseases. Unilateral vision loss can be observed, particularly if the other eye is already blind. Lesions of the optic nerve can cause blindness, as the disease process can be localized. Ophthalmic emergencies require rapid diagnosis and appropriate therapy for maintenance of vision.

Chapter: Ophthalmology, Emergency

Source: The Merck Veterinary Manual, 11th Edition (Page 1700)

faqs.question: What are common causes of sudden blindness in one eye?
faqs.answer: Common causes include acute glaucoma (high eye pressure), retinal detachment, sudden acquired retinal degeneration syndrome (SARDS), or severe internal inflammation known as uveitis.

faqs.question: Can my dog still lead a normal life if the blindness is permanent?
faqs.answer: Yes, most dogs adapt very well to vision loss in one eye, provided the underlying cause is managed and any associated pain is controlled by a veterinarian.

faqs.question: Will my dog show signs of pain with sudden blindness?
faqs.answer: Often, yes. Signs of eye pain include squinting, pawing at the face, excessive tearing, or becoming unusually lethargic or withdrawn.
